    # --- Auth & Account schemas ---

    Enterprise:
      type: object
      required: [id, name, slug, plan, billing_email, created_at, updated_at]
      properties:
        id:
          type: string
          examples: [ent_abc123]
        name:
          type: string
        slug:
          type: string
          description: URL-friendly unique identifier
        plan:
          type: string
          enum: [free, pro, enterprise]
        personal:
          type: boolean
          default: false
          description: True for single-user personal accounts (e.g. Gmail sign-in)
        billing_email:
          type: string
          format: email
        sso_required:
          type: boolean
          default: false
        allowed_auth_providers:
          type: array
          items:
            type: string
          description: Restrict sign-in to these providers. Empty means all allowed.
        allowed_email_domains:
          type: array
          items:
            type: string
          description: Restrict sign-in to these email domains. Empty means all allowed.
        created_at:
          type: string
          format: date-time
        updated_at:
          type: string
          format: date-time

    User:
      type: object
      description: |
        A user account. The id is an opaque surrogate key (usr_ + UUID). Email is
        the stable logical identity used to deduplicate across OAuth providers.
      required: [id, enterprise_id, email, display_name, role, status, auth_provider, created_at, updated_at]
      properties:
        id:
          type: string
          description: Opaque surrogate key — immutable, never changes
          examples: [usr_abc123]
        enterprise_id:
          type: string
        email:
          type: string
          format: email
          description: Unique email — the stable identity across OAuth providers
        display_name:
          type: string
        avatar_url:
          type: string
          format: uri
        role:
          type: string
          enum: [owner, admin, member]
        status:
          type: string
          enum: [active, pending_verification, invited, suspended]
        auth_provider:
          type: string
          description: Identity provider used for authentication (e.g. google, github, saml)
        last_login_at:
          type: string
          format: date-time
          nullable: true
        created_at:
          type: string
          format: date-time
        updated_at:
          type: string
          format: date-time

    RefreshTokenRequest:
      type: object
      properties:
        refresh_token:
          type: string
          description: Refresh token (alternatively sent via httpOnly cookie)

    RefreshTokenResponse:
      type: object
      required: [access_token]
      properties:
        access_token:
          type: string

    LogoutResponse:
      type: object
      required: [status]
      properties:
        status:
          type: string
          enum: [logged_out]

    UpdateUserRequest:
      type: object
      properties:
        display_name:
          type: string

    UpdateEnterpriseRequest:
      type: object
      properties:
        name:
          type: string
        billing_email:
          type: string
          format: email
        sso_required:
          type: boolean
        allowed_auth_providers:
          type: array
          items:
            type: string
          description: Restrict sign-in to these providers. Empty array means all allowed.
        allowed_email_domains:
          type: array
          items:
            type: string
          description: Restrict sign-in to these email domains. Empty array means all allowed.

    SignupRequest:
      type: object
      required: [email, password]
      properties:
        email:
          type: string
          format: email
        password:
          type: string
          minLength: 8
        display_name:
          type: string
          description: Defaults to the email username if omitted

    SignupResponse:
      type: object
      required: [user_id, status, message]
      properties:
        user_id:
          type: string
        status:
          type: string
          enum: [pending_verification]
        message:
          type: string

    VerifyEmailRequest:
      type: object
      required: [email, code]
      properties:
        email:
          type: string
          format: email
        code:
          type: string
          description: 6-digit verification code

    VerifyEmailResponse:
      type: object
      required: [status, message]
      properties:
        status:
          type: string
          enum: [verified]
        message:
          type: string

    EmailLoginRequest:
      type: object
      required: [email, password]
      properties:
        email:
          type: string
          format: email
        password:
          type: string

1→// Package store defines persistence interfaces for the control plane.
     2→//
     3→// These interfaces decouple the API handlers from the storage backend.
     4→// The in-memory implementations (in the mem sub-package) are suitable for
     5→// development and testing. Production deployments swap in Postgres-backed
     6→// implementations without changing business logic.
     7→package store
     8→
     9→import (
    10→	"context"
    11→
    12→	api "github.com/ALRubinger/aileron/core/api/gen"
    13→	"github.com/ALRubinger/aileron/core/model"
    14→)
    15→
    16→// IntentStore persists and retrieves action intents.
    17→type IntentStore interface {
    18→	Create(ctx context.Context, intent api.IntentEnvelope) error
    19→	Get(ctx context.Context, intentID string) (api.IntentEnvelope, error)
    20→	List(ctx context.Context, filter IntentFilter) ([]api.IntentEnvelope, error)
    21→	Update(ctx context.Context, intent api.IntentEnvelope) error
    22→}
    23→
    24→// IntentFilter scopes an intent list query.
    25→type IntentFilter struct {
    26→	WorkspaceID string
    27→	Status      *api.IntentStatus
    28→	AgentID     string
    29→	PageSize    int
    30→	PageToken   string
    31→}
    32→
    33→// ApprovalStore persists and retrieves approval requests.
    34→type ApprovalStore interface {
    35→	Create(ctx context.Context, approval api.Approval) error
    36→	Get(ctx context.Context, approvalID string) (api.Approval, error)
    37→	List(ctx context.Context, filter ApprovalFilter) ([]api.Approval, error)
    38→	Update(ctx context.Context, approval api.Approval) error
    39→}
    40→
    41→// ApprovalFilter scopes an approval list query.
    42→type ApprovalFilter struct {
    43→	WorkspaceID string
    44→	Status      *api.ApprovalStatus
    45→	IntentID    string
    46→	PageSize    int
    47→	PageToken   string
    48→}
    49→
    50→// PolicyStore persists and retrieves policy definitions.
    51→type PolicyStore interface {
    52→	Create(ctx context.Context, policy api.Policy) error
    53→	Get(ctx context.Context, policyID string) (api.Policy, error)
    54→	List(ctx context.Context, filter PolicyFilter) ([]api.Policy, error)
    55→	Update(ctx context.Context, policy api.Policy) error
    56→}
    57→
    58→// PolicyFilter scopes a policy list query.
    59→type PolicyFilter struct {
    60→	WorkspaceID string
    61→	Status      *api.PolicyStatus
    62→	PageSize    int
    63→	PageToken   string
    64→}
    65→
    66→// GrantStore persists and retrieves execution grants.
    67→type GrantStore interface {
    68→	Create(ctx context.Context, grant api.ExecutionGrant) error
    69→	Get(ctx context.Context, grantID string) (api.ExecutionGrant, error)
    70→	Update(ctx context.Context, grant api.ExecutionGrant) error
    71→}
    72→
    73→// ExecutionStore persists and retrieves execution records.
    74→type ExecutionStore interface {
    75→	Create(ctx context.Context, execution api.Execution) error
    76→	Get(ctx context.Context, executionID string) (api.Execution, error)
    77→	Update(ctx context.Context, execution api.Execution) error
    78→}
    79→
    80→// ConnectorStore persists and retrieves connector registrations.
    81→type ConnectorStore interface {
    82→	Create(ctx context.Context, connector api.Connector) error
    83→	Get(ctx context.Context, connectorID string) (api.Connector, error)
    84→	List(ctx context.Context, filter ConnectorFilter) ([]api.Connector, error)
    85→	Update(ctx context.Context, connector api.Connector) error
    86→}
    87→
    88→// ConnectorFilter scopes a connector list query.
    89→type ConnectorFilter struct {
    90→	WorkspaceID string
    91→	Type        *api.ConnectorType
    92→	PageSize    int
    93→	PageToken   string
    94→}
    95→
    96→// CredentialStore persists and retrieves credential references (not secrets).
    97→type CredentialStore interface {
    98→	Create(ctx context.Context, cred api.CredentialReference) error
    99→	Get(ctx context.Context, credentialID string) (api.CredentialReference, error)
   100→	List(ctx context.Context, filter CredentialFilter) ([]api.CredentialReference, error)
   101→}
   102→

   144→	Update(ctx context.Context, enterprise model.Enterprise) error

   170→	GetByRefreshTokenHash(ctx context.Context, refreshHash string) (model.Session, error)

   179→	GetActiveByUserID(ctx context.Context, userID string) (model.VerificationCode, error)
   180→	MarkUsed(ctx context.Context, codeID string) error
   181→	DeleteExpiredForUser(ctx context.Context, userID string) error
   182→}
   183→
   184→// SSOConfigStore persists and retrieves per-enterprise SSO configurations.
   185→type SSOConfigStore interface {

package app

import (
	"context"
	"encoding/json"
	"errors"
	"fmt"
	"log/slog"
	"net/http"
	"sort"
	"time"

	api "github.com/ALRubinger/aileron/core/api/gen"
	"github.com/ALRubinger/aileron/core/approval"
	connectorpkg "github.com/ALRubinger/aileron/core/connector"
	"github.com/ALRubinger/aileron/core/model"
	"github.com/ALRubinger/aileron/core/notify"
	"github.com/ALRubinger/aileron/core/policy"
	"github.com/ALRubinger/aileron/core/registry"
	"github.com/ALRubinger/aileron/core/store"
	"github.com/ALRubinger/aileron/core/store/mem"
	"github.com/ALRubinger/aileron/core/vault"
	"github.com/ALRubinger/aileron/core/version"
)

// apiServer implements the generated api.ServerInterface.
type apiServer struct {
	log            *slog.Logger
	registry       *connectorpkg.Registry
	policyEngine   *policy.RuleEngine
	orchestrator   *approval.InMemoryOrchestrator
	vault          *vault.MemVault
	notifier       notify.Notifier
	intents        *mem.IntentStore
	approvals      *mem.ApprovalStore
	policies       *mem.PolicyStore
	grants         *mem.GrantStore
	executions     *mem.ExecutionStore
	connectors     *mem.ConnectorStore
	mcpServers     *mem.MCPServerStore
	registryClient *registry.Client
	credentials    *mem.CredentialStore
	fundingSources *mem.FundingSourceStore
	traces         *mem.TraceStore
	enterprises    store.EnterpriseStore // nil when auth is disabled
	users          store.UserStore       // nil when auth is disabled
	newID          func() string
}

// --- JSON helpers ---

func writeJSON(w http.ResponseWriter, status int, v any) {
	w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "application/json")
	w.WriteHeader(status)
	json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(v)
}

func writeError(w http.ResponseWriter, status int, code, message string) {
	writeJSON(w, status, api.Error{
		Error: struct {

	registryClient       *registry.Client

content.text:
Here's a summary of everything that was implemented:

## Summary

### OpenAPI Spec (`core/api/openapi.yaml`)
- Added `user_id` (readOnly) and `source` (enum: personal/enterprise, readOnly) to `MCPServerConfig`
- Added new `EnterpriseMCPServer` schema with `enterprise_id`, `auto_enabled`, and all standard server config fields
- Added 6 new enterprise endpoints under `/v1/enterprise/mcp-servers` (CRUD + credentials)
- Added 401/403 error responses to all MCP and marketplace endpoints

### Store SPI (`core/store/store.go`)
- Added `UserID string` to `MCPServerFilter`
- Added `EnterpriseMCPServerStore` interface and `EnterpriseMCPServerFilter` (with `EnterpriseID`, `AutoEnabled`)

### In-Memory Stores
- **Modified** `core/store/mem/mcpserver.go` — `List()` filters by `UserID` when set
- **New** `core/store/mem/enterprise_mcpserver.go` — full CRUD with enterprise/auto-enabled filtering

### Handlers (`core/app/handlers.go`)
- Added helper functions: `userIDFromRequest`, `enterpriseIDFromRequest`, `isAdmin`, `requireAuth`
- `requireAuth` gracefully falls back when auth is disabled (local dev)
- All existing MCP handlers now enforce auth and user ownership
- `ListMCPServers` merges personal servers with enterprise auto-enabled servers, tagging each with `source`
- `ListMarketplaceServers` `installed` flag now reflects both personal and enterprise auto-enabled
- 6 new enterprise MCP handlers with admin-only write enforcement

### Tests
- **Store**: Added user-scoped filtering test, plus 4 enterprise store tests (CRUD, not-found, enterprise isolation, auto-enabled filtering)
- **Handlers**: 7 new tests covering user scoping, enterprise merging, ownership checks, admin-only enforcement, enterprise isolation, and auth enforcement

### UI
- `api.ts`: Added 6 enterprise MCP server API functions
- `servers/+page.svelte`: Source badge (Personal/Enterprise), hide delete for enterprise servers
- `servers/[id]/+page.svelte`: Source badge, hide credential form and danger zone for enterprise servers
- `settings/+layout.svelte`: Added "MCP Servers" nav link
- **New** `settings/mcp-servers/+page.svelte`: Admin page for managing enterprise approved list (add/remove servers, toggle auto-enable)

message.content: Tests for everything new? All tests pass? Code coverage is above 80% and not lower than it was before? Make an ADR to supercede any prior ones on the user/Enterprise model. Update the README as necessary.

1→# Aileron
     2→_Stay on course. The missing protection layer between your agents and the real world._
     3→
     4→![GitHub License](https://img.shields.io/github/license/ALRubinger/aileron?style=for-the-badge)
     5→![GitHub Actions Workflow Status](https://img.shields.io/github/actions/workflow/status/ALRubinger/aileron/ci.yml?style=for-the-badge&logo=github)
     6→
     7→**Aileron is an MCP gateway that enforces governance over every tool call an AI agent makes.**
     8→
     9→It sits between an agent host and the MCP servers the agent uses. Aileron aggregates downstream MCP servers, re-exposes their tools, and intercepts every tool call for policy evaluation — without the agent ever touching credentials directly.
    10→
    11→---
    12→
    13→## The Problem
    14→
    15→AI agents are acting on our behalf: booking meetings, paying invoices, merging code. The problem isn't capability. It's trust. An agent that's useful is an agent that's risky.
    16→
    17→The current workarounds are inadequate:
    18→
    19→- Hardcoded rules buried inside agents, invisible to the people they affect
    20→- Manual approvals handled out-of-band in Slack or email, disconnected from execution
    21→- Over-permissioned API keys that agents hold directly
    22→- Static payment credentials exposed to systems that shouldn't see them
    23→- Agents can bypass governance tools entirely if they have direct access to APIs or other MCP servers
    24→
    25→The result is a forced choice: give the agent enough permission to be powerful, or restrict it enough to feel safe. Neither is satisfying.
    26→
    27→## The Solution
    28→
    29→Aileron is the **single MCP server** that an agent host connects to. It aggregates downstream MCP servers, re-exposes their tools under namespaced names, and intercepts every tool call for policy evaluation. The agent can't bypass Aileron because Aileron IS the tool surface.
    30→
    31→Agents remain autonomous in planning. We retain authority over execution. Aileron enforces the boundary between the two — by construction, not by cooperation.
    32→
    33→```
    34→Agent Host (Claude Code, OpenClaw, etc.)
    35→  │
    36→  │  MCP (stdio)
    37→  │
    38→  ▼
    39→Aileron MCP Gateway
    40→  ├── Policy Engine         evaluates rules per tool call
    41→  ├── Approval Orchestrator routes to humans when required
    42→  ├── Credential Vault      injects secrets at launch time
    43→  └── Audit Store           immutable record of every decision
    44→  │
    45→  ├──► Downstream MCP Server A (e.g. GitHub)
    46→  ├──► Downstream MCP Server B (e.g. Stripe)
    47→  └──► Downstream MCP Server C (e.g. Slack)
    48→```
    49→
    50→## How It Works
    51→
    52→**1. Agent host connects to Aileron as its only MCP server**
    53→
    54→Claude Code, OpenClaw, or any MCP-compatible agent host is configured with Aileron as the sole MCP server. The agent sees only the tools that Aileron exposes.
    55→
    56→**2. Aileron discovers tools from downstream MCP servers**
    57→
    58→On startup, Aileron connects to configured downstream MCP servers, discovers their tools, and re-exposes them under namespaced names (e.g. `github__create_pull_request`).
    59→
    60→**3. Every tool call passes through policy evaluation**
    61→
    62→When the agent calls a tool, Aileron intercepts it, maps it to the policy engine, and evaluates it against configured rules. The disposition is allow, deny, require approval, or allow with modifications.
    63→
    64→**4. Humans approve high-risk actions**
    65→
    66→If approval is required, Aileron holds the tool call and notifies approvers. The agent can poll with `aileron__check_approval`. When approved, Aileron auto-executes the queued call and returns the real result.
    67→
    68→**5. Credentials are injected from the vault**
    69→
    70→Downstream MCP servers receive credentials from the Aileron vault at launch time. Agents never see API keys, tokens, or secrets.
    71→
    72→**6. Everything is logged**
    73→
    74→Every tool call interception, policy decision, approval, and execution is recorded in an immutable audit trail. You have a verifiable record of what every agent did, who approved it, and when.
    75→
    76→## For Organizations
    77→
    78→Aileron gives organizations centralized control over agent activity across teams.
    79→
    80→- **Service catalog.** Configure which MCP servers are available to employees. Agents only see the tools you expose.
    81→- **Credential management.** API keys, tokens, and secrets live in the Aileron vault. Teams use agents without handling credentials directly.
    82→- **Policy governance.** Define rules that apply across all agent activity — spend limits, branch protections, vendor allowlists, time-of-day controls.
    83→- **Compliance.** An immutable audit trail records every tool call, policy decision, and approval for review and export.
    84→
    85→## Configuration
    86→
    87→Aileron is configured with an `aileron.yaml` file that declares downstream MCP servers, credential references, and policy mappings.
    88→
    89→```yaml
    90→version: "1"
    91→downstream_servers:
    92→  - name: "github"
    93→    command: ["npx", "-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-github"]
    94→    env:
    95→      GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN: "vault://connectors/github/default"
    96→    policy_mapping:
    97→      tool_prefix: "git"
    98→  - name: "filesystem"
    99→    command: ["npx", "-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem", "/workspace"]
   100→```

1→# ADR-0001: Hybrid MCP Execution Model
     2→
     3→**Status:** Proposed
     4→**Date:** 2026-04-01
     5→
     6→## Context
     7→
     8→Aileron is an MCP gateway/aggregator — the single MCP server an agent host connects to. It proxies downstream MCP servers, intercepting every `tools/call` for policy evaluation, approval gating, credential injection, and audit logging.
     9→
    10→Today, the gateway spawns each downstream MCP server as a **local subprocess** communicating over stdio (newline-delimited JSON-RPC 2.0). The YAML configuration defines a `command` and optional `env` per server. This model works well for single-user, local-first operation.
    11→
    12→For enterprise deployment, Aileron's control plane (policy engine, approval orchestration, vault, audit log) is cloud-hosted. This raises a fundamental question: **where do the downstream MCP servers run?**
    13→
    14→Three deployment models were considered:
    15→
    16→### Fully centralized
    17→
    18→All downstream MCP servers run alongside the control plane in the cloud. Users connect their agent hosts to a remote Aileron gateway.
    19→
    20→**Problems:**
    21→- MCP servers that need local context (filesystem access, IDE integrations, local dev tools) cannot run remotely.
    22→- Centralizing N users × M servers creates multi-tenant sandboxing, resource scaling, and noisy-neighbor challenges.
    23→- Aileron becomes a managed execution platform — a fundamentally different product scope.
    24→
    25→### Fully local
    26→
    27→The gateway and all downstream MCP servers run on each user's machine. The control plane is remote, but tool execution is entirely local.
    28→
    29→**Problems:**
    30→- Every user must install and configure every MCP server locally, including dependencies (Node.js runtimes, Python environments, etc.).

1→# ADR-0002: MCP Marketplace Integration
     2→
     3→**Status:** Accepted
     4→**Date:** 2026-04-01
     5→
     6→## Context
     7→
     8→Aileron's value proposition depends on being the first thing developers install when working with AI agents. Today, configuring downstream MCP servers requires hand-editing `aileron.yaml` — the developer must know the server's npm package name, its command-line arguments, and which environment variables it needs. This friction works against the goal of making Aileron the natural starting point for agent-based workflows.
     9→
    10→The MCP ecosystem has matured to include an **official MCP Registry** (`registry.modelcontextprotocol.io`) backed by Anthropic, GitHub, and Microsoft. The registry exposes a public REST API (`GET /v0/servers`) returning server metadata, package coordinates, and required environment variables. Third-party directories (Smithery, Glama, PulseMCP) also exist, each with varying API surfaces and data enrichments.
    11→
    12→Three approaches were considered for enabling click-to-install MCP server management through Aileron:
    13→
    14→### Build a proprietary catalog
    15→
    16→Aileron maintains its own curated server catalog with metadata, installation scripts, and compatibility ratings.
    17→
    18→**Problems:**
    19→- Duplicates effort already invested in the official registry.
    20→- Requires ongoing curation and maintenance of server metadata.
    21→- Fragments the ecosystem — server authors must publish to yet another registry.
    22→- Delays launch: the catalog must reach critical mass before it's useful.
    23→
    24→### Aggregate multiple registries
    25→
    26→Proxy the official registry plus Smithery, Glama, and PulseMCP to maximize coverage.
    27→
    28→**Problems:**
    29→- Each registry has a different API shape, authentication model, and data schema.
    30→- Deduplication across sources is non-trivial (same server, different metadata).

1→# ADR-0003: Marketplace Dashboard UI
     2→
     3→**Status:** Accepted
     4→**Date:** 2026-04-02
     5→
     6→## Context
     7→
     8→ADR-0002 established the backend API for MCP marketplace integration — registry proxy, one-click install, CRUD on installed servers, and vault-backed credential storage. That ADR explicitly deferred the dashboard UI as a follow-up PR.
     9→
    10→Without a visual interface, developers must interact with the marketplace through `curl` or scripts. This defeats the goal of making Aileron the frictionless first-install for agent workflows. A dashboard that matches the existing Aileron UI patterns (approvals, traces, policies) completes the marketplace feature as a usable product surface.
    11→
    12→Two approaches were considered for the UI structure:
    13→
    14→### Single page with tabs
    15→
    16→A single `/servers` page combining marketplace browsing and installed server management in a tabbed layout.
    17→
    18→**Problems:**
    19→- Conflates two distinct workflows: discovery (browsing/searching) vs. management (configuring/monitoring).
    20→- Tab state is lost on navigation, making it harder to deep-link or share URLs.
    21→- The marketplace server list can be large; mixing it with the smaller installed-servers list creates visual noise.
    22→
    23→### Separate pages (selected)
    24→
    25→Dedicated routes for marketplace browsing (`/marketplace`) and installed server management (`/servers`, `/servers/{id}`), each with focused UX.
    26→
    27→**Problems:**
    28→- Slightly more files to maintain.
    29→- Users must navigate between pages when installing then configuring.
    30→

1→# ADR-0004: Enterprise Account Model, SSO/OAuth, and Email/Password Auth
     2→
     3→**Status:** Superseded by [ADR-0005](0005-github-oauth-and-auth-spi-evolution.md)
     4→**Date:** 2026-04-02
     5→
     6→## Context
     7→
     8→Aileron had no authentication, no user model, and no persistent database. The OpenAPI spec declared Bearer JWT auth, but no validation existed — all endpoints were open. The control plane used an abstract `ActorRef` (agent/human/service) with no concept of user accounts, organizations, or sessions.
     9→
    10→For the hosted cloud offering, Aileron needs:
    11→
    12→- **Multi-user accounts** with centralized billing, settings, and user management.
    13→- **SSO and OAuth** so users can sign in with identity providers their organization already uses.
    14→- **Email/password signup** as a baseline auth method that works without any external provider.
    15→- **A real database** to persist users, sessions, and enterprise configuration.
    16→
    17→The `saas/multitenancy/`, `saas/billing/`, and `saas/infra/` directories existed as empty placeholders, signaling intent but no implementation.
    18→
    19→## Decision
    20→
    21→### Enterprise as the top-level account
    22→
    23→We introduced **Enterprise** as the top-level organizational entity. Every user belongs to exactly one Enterprise (1:1 relationship). Enterprises own workspaces, billing, SSO configuration, and user management.
    24→
    25→**Personal vs. organizational Enterprises:** When a user signs up or signs in with a personal email domain (Gmail, Yahoo, Outlook, Proton, iCloud, etc.), a **personal Enterprise** is auto-created (`personal: true`). Users with organizational email domains (e.g. `alice@acme.com`) get an organizational Enterprise. This distinction allows the UI and billing to treat single-user hobby accounts differently from team accounts without requiring a separate account type.
    26→
    27→**Why 1:1 (not many-to-many):** A user belonging to multiple Enterprises (like Slack workspaces) was considered but rejected for simplicity. The added complexity of a membership join table, role-per-enterprise, and enterprise switching UI was not justified by current requirements. This can be revisited when multi-org support is needed.
    28→
    29→### Surrogate primary key for users
    30→

1→# ADR-0005: GitHub OAuth Provider and Auth SPI Evolution
     2→
     3→**Status:** Accepted
     4→**Date:** 2026-04-03
     5→**Supersedes:** [ADR-0004](0004-enterprise-auth-sso.md) (Enterprise Account Model, SSO/OAuth, and Email/Password Auth)
     6→
     7→## Context
     8→
     9→ADR-0004 established the auth SPI, enterprise model, and Google OAuth as the first provider. The system was designed for pluggable providers, but only one existed. Adding a second provider (GitHub) surfaced two gaps in the SPI and one unnecessary Google-specific coupling in the handler configuration.
    10→
    11→Additionally, X.com (Twitter) was evaluated as a third provider. Its OAuth 2.0 API requires PKCE and does not provide user email — a field the system relies on for cross-provider deduplication, enterprise creation, and domain enforcement. X.com was deferred, but the SPI needed to evolve to support PKCE for when it is eventually added.
    12→
    13→## Decision
    14→
    15→### SPI evolution: AuthorizationResult and ExtraState
    16→
    17→The `AuthProvider.AuthorizationURL` method now returns `*AuthorizationResult` instead of a bare URL string:
    18→
    19→```go
    20→type AuthorizationResult struct {
    21→    URL        string
    22→    ExtraState string // opaque, persisted by handler across redirect
    23→}
    24→
    25→type CallbackRequest struct {
    26→    Code        string
    27→    State       string
    28→    RedirectURL string
    29→    ExtraState  string // from AuthorizationResult, if any
    30→}

1→# ADR-0006: Stable OAuth Callback Domain for Branch Deployments
     2→
     3→**Status:** Superseded by [ADR-0007](0007-remove-stable-oauth-callback-domain.md)
     4→**Date:** 2026-04-03
     5→**Supersedes:** ADR-0005 (specifically the "Removal of OAuthRedirectURL override" decision)
     6→
     7→## Context
     8→
     9→ADR-0005 removed the static `OAuthRedirectURL` config field and `GOOGLE_REDIRECT_URL` environment variable, arguing that fully dynamic callback URL derivation from the incoming request host was sufficient for all deployment topologies.
    10→
    11→This assumption holds for single-domain deployments and local development, but breaks under Railway's branch deployment model. Railway gives every branch deploy a unique, unpredictable hostname (e.g. `feat-foo-abc123.up.railway.app`). OAuth providers (Google, GitHub) require all callback URLs to be pre-registered in their developer consoles. The dynamic derivation produces a new URL per branch, requiring the operator to manually add each branch hostname to the provider's allowed redirect URIs — not scalable for ephemeral branches.
    12→
    13→The root constraint is not in Aileron's code but in the OAuth provider's callback URL whitelist. The solution must reduce the set of registered URIs to one stable URL while preserving full auth functionality on any number of branch deployments.
    14→
    15→## Decision
    16→
    17→### Stable callback domain as OAuth relay
    18→
    19→A single stable domain (e.g. `https://auth.withaileron.ai`) is designated to receive all OAuth callbacks. It is configured as the only registered redirect URI in each OAuth provider's console. Branch deployments route their OAuth flows through this stable domain via a relay mechanism.
    20→
    21→This is a configuration-driven approach: no new binary, no new service, no infrastructure change. The stable domain is simply an Aileron deployment — the same Docker image, the same code — running on a domain that never changes.
    22→
    23→### OAuth state encodes the originating host
    24→
    25→When a branch deployment initiates an OAuth login and `AILERON_OAUTH_CALLBACK_BASE_URL` is set to the stable domain, the auth handler encodes the originating branch hostname into the OAuth state parameter:
    26→
    27→```
    28→state = "{32-hex-csrf-token}.{base64url(originating_host)}"
    29→```
    30→

1→# ADR-0007: Remove Stable OAuth Callback Domain
     2→
     3→**Status:** Accepted
     4→**Date:** 2026-04-03
     5→**Supersedes:** ADR-0006
     6→
     7→## Context
     8→
     9→ADR-0006 introduced a stable `auth.withaileron.ai` subdomain to serve as an OAuth callback relay for Railway branch deployments. This allowed branch deploys with unpredictable hostnames to complete OAuth flows through a single registered redirect URI.
    10→
    11→In practice, this added significant complexity (state encoding, relay redirects, trusted origins validation) for a feature that didn't work reliably with Railway's generated hostnames. The relay mechanism was removed in commit 83ee28d, and branch deploy users authenticate via email/password instead.
    12→
    13→With the relay removed, the `auth.withaileron.ai` subdomain serves no purpose — it points to the same Railway service as `api.withaileron.ai` and has no distinct routing behavior.
    14→
    15→## Decision
    16→
    17→Remove the `auth.withaileron.ai` subdomain entirely. All API and auth traffic uses `api.withaileron.ai`. OAuth callback URLs registered with providers become:
    18→
    19→- **Google:** `https://api.withaileron.ai/auth/google/callback`
    20→- **GitHub:** `https://api.withaileron.ai/auth/github/callback`
    21→
    22→The callback URL derivation in `callbackURL()` remains fully dynamic from the incoming request host — no configuration needed.
    23→
    24→## Consequences
    25→
    26→- One fewer DNS record and Railway custom domain to maintain.
    27→- OAuth provider consoles reference the same domain as the API.
    28→- Branch deployments continue to work for non-OAuth auth (email/password).
    29→- The `AILERON_OAUTH_CALLBACK_BASE_URL` and `AILERON_TRUSTED_ORIGINS` env vars (already removed in code) are no longer documented.
    30→
74:Every tool call interception, policy decision, approval, and execution is recorded in an immutable audit trail. You have a verifiable record of what every agent did, who approved it, and when.
